evidence: Dependency seams are modeled as many embedded function-bag structs with overlapping responsibilities (for example `RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, `RuntimeSnapshotOps`, `RuntimeConfigOps`) and then assembled in `buildRuntimeSeamBundle`, creating high indirection before reaching real behavior.

Multiple modules repeat the same pattern of global default runtime + override + nil-guard validation (`defaultRectifyRuntime`/`NewRectifyRuntimeWithDependencies`, `defaultImporterRuntime`/`ensureForWorkflow`, `newWorkflowRuntime`/`withDefaultsWorkflowRuntime`) rather than sharing a stable boundary abstraction.

Global mutable singleton entrypoints (`SetEventBroker`, package-level broadcast state/runtime globals, and package-level wifi state/runtime globals) create hidden dependency channels that bypass explicit construction and make abstractions less honest about required collaborators.

README.md defines contract quality gates and requires explicit lifecycle-policy assertions in `goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts_test.go`, but `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go` and `goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go` contain only static catalog data with no embedded executable invariants that enforce those test obligations.
All contract metadata in scoped files pins `IntroducedIn` to `CurrentContractVersion`, which removes historical-version anchors that tests could use to validate compatibility behavior over time.

`protocol_contracts.go` duplicates contract identity across multiple constants and catalog entries (ID/namespace/action/descriptor), increasing omission risk unless there is a strict table-driven test harness derived from one source of truth.

Function-bag runtime structs are repeated across modules (`Runtime*Ops`, `collectorRuntime`, `NetdataRuntime`, `wifiRuntime`) and rely on pervasive nil-guard wrappers instead of explicit capability boundaries.
Session/logstream API is mirrored at multiple layers: `logstreamSessionStore` and `sessionState` duplicate nearly identical method surfaces and pass-through behavior.
`collectors.go` centralizes backup snapshotting, urbit composition, profile assembly, and system hardware collection in one large file with cross-domain imports and mixed concerns.

findings.summary: Function-bag runtime abstractions are fragmented across modules, adding indirection without a cohesive domain boundary.
evidence: `runtime_ops.go` defines many embedded ops structs with mostly direct function pointers to package functions, then composes them in `buildRuntimeSeamBundle`.
`rectify.go` mirrors the same shape (`rectifyConfigRuntime`, `rectifyContainerRuntime`, `rectifyUrbitConfigRuntime`, `rectifyPersistenceRuntime`) and merges via `seams.Merge`.
`importer.go` defines a large `importerRuntime` with many callbacks and a long `ensureForWorkflow` nil-check chain.
`operations_runtime.go` repeats another local runtime callback aggregate with defaults and per-call fallback wrapping.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a smaller set of capability-oriented interfaces per workflow boundary (for example: transition store, ship config repository, container control service), and move nil-guard/default construction into shared constructors so each module consumes a compact, behavior-focused dependency object instead of bespoke callback bags.

findings.summary: Global mutable runtime singletons create hidden coupling that bypasses explicit dependency boundaries.
evidence: `events.go` exposes package-level `defaultBroker` with `SetEventBroker`, while publish/subscribe functions read it implicitly.
`state.go` keeps package-global mutable state and event bus (`broadcastState`, `schedulePackBus`) accessed through package functions.
`wifi.go` keeps package-global runtime state (`WifiInfo`, `Device`, `c2cEnabled`) with runtime indirection layered on top.
`collectors.go` caches `defaultCollectorRuntimeValue` globally and uses it in exported collectors, coupling behavior to package state rather than explicit wiring.
findings.suggestion: Replace package-global mutable singletons with explicit runtime instances passed through startup wiring, and expose constructors that return scoped services (event broker, broadcast store, wifi service, collector service) to make dependency ownership explicit and test seams honest.

findings.summary: Descriptor/callback wrapper layers frequently forward functions without adding policy, increasing call depth for little leverage.
evidence: `startup_orchestrator.go` has descriptor builders that mostly return runtime function fields directly, followed by additional step wrappers (`startupSubsystemAction`, required/optional wrappers).
`main.go` defines nested runtime structs (`bootstrapRuntime`, `c2cRuntime*`) and wrapper defaults that mainly forward to existing package functions.
`operations_runtime.go` wraps transition emission/dispatch through multiple helper layers (`RunTransitionedOperation` -> `runTransitionedOperation` -> `runTransitionLifecycle` -> runtime emit).
findings.suggestion: Collapse pass-through wrapper tiers where no cross-cutting policy is enforced, and keep indirection only at true boundary points (retry, timeout, telemetry, fallback) so each abstraction level contributes measurable behavior.

findings.summary: Configuration abstraction exposes a very wide mutation surface, leaking persistence details into orchestration logic.
evidence: `runtime_ops.go` injects many granular config mutation callbacks (`UpdateUrbit*`, `UpdateConfTypedFn`, etc.), pushing low-level config API breadth into runtime seams.

`rectify_services.go` coordinates remote reconciliation by mutating many `UrbitDocker` fields and deciding which persistence function to invoke (`UpdateUrbitWebConfigFn`, `UpdateUrbitNetworkConfigFn`).
`config_update_registry.go` centralizes a large field registry, indicating many caller-visible patch points across unrelated concerns.
`structs/configs.go` contains broad config aggregates that encourage cross-domain read/write from orchestration code.
findings.suggestion: Introduce domain-specific config services (for example StartramSyncConfigService, UrbitNetworkConfigService) that expose intent-level operations and hide field-level patch mechanics; then route orchestration through those services instead of directly handling many update callbacks and field mappings.

findings.summary: Contract quality-gate rules are documented but not enforced by executable invariants in the contract catalog sources.
evidence: README.md requires explicit lifecycle-policy assertions for contract catalog changes.

The scoped Go files expose only literal catalog entries and metadata; there is no self-checking mechanism (e.g., required test vectors, completeness assertions, or compile-time guard patterns) tied to those governance requirements.
findings.suggestion: Add a contract-test manifest generated from catalog entries (or a shared table exported by catalog code) and enforce it in tests/CI so any new or modified contract entry fails fast unless lifecycle and compatibility assertions are updated.

findings.summary: Using `CurrentContractVersion` for all scoped contracts removes stable anchors for regression tests of compatibility history.
evidence: `uploadActionOpenEndpointIntroducedIn`, `uploadActionResetIntroducedIn`, `c2cConnectActionIntroducedIn`, and `startramApiConnectionErrorIntroducedIn` are all set to `CurrentContractVersion`.
Without fixed historical version points in contract definitions, tests cannot reliably detect accidental rewrites of introduction/version history semantics.
findings.suggestion: Pin `IntroducedIn` values to explicit semantic versions at introduction time and add regression tests that assert version history and compatibility policy do not drift when `CurrentContractVersion` changes.

findings.summary: Manual duplication of contract identity fields increases the chance of partial updates that tests miss.
evidence: Each protocol contract repeats identity data across constants (`ContractID`, `ActionToken`, `ActionNamespace`) and catalog entry fields (`ID`, `Namespace`, `Action`, `Descriptor.Name`).
README.md positions these files as contract surfaces under CI policy, but the duplication pattern creates multiple mutation points that require broad test coverage to catch mismatch cases.
findings.suggestion: Refactor to a single canonical contract spec structure and derive constants/bindings/descriptors from it, then add table-driven tests that validate ID-token-namespace-descriptor consistency for every generated entry.

findings.summary: Runtime dependency wiring is scattered into oversized function-bag structs that force repeated guard/wrapper code.
evidence: `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` defines many granular `*Ops` structs with callback fields (e.g., lines 30-169), creating a broad, fragmented seam surface.

`goseg/broadcast/collectors/collectors.go` defines a 17-field `collectorRuntime` (lines 35-53) plus `ensureConfigured` and per-callback wrappers (lines 183-349) to compensate for optional dependencies.

`goseg/docker/orchestration/container/netdata.go` repeats the same pattern with a large runtime struct (lines 16-36) and repeated nil checks in each operation path (lines 40-48, 69-71, 120-126, 174-186).
findings.suggestion: Replace broad callback bags with a few cohesive interfaces per use-case (e.g., config IO, container lifecycle, runtime snapshot), require them at construction time, and remove per-method nil fallbacks.

findings.summary: Session/logstream behavior is duplicated across layers, creating pass-through bloat and drift risk.
evidence: `goseg/session/session.go` duplicates logstream operations in both `logstreamSessionStore` (lines 40-201) and `sessionState` pass-through methods (lines 241-323) with mostly identical method coverage.

`goseg/logger/logger.go` has duplicated sink configuration entry points (`ConfigureLogstreamRuntime` lines 125-133 and `configureSystemLogSink` lines 205-213) that implement the same state mutation logic.
findings.suggestion: Define a single owner for logstream/session mutation and expose one minimal API; remove pass-through mirror methods and consolidate sink configuration into a single function.

findings.summary: `collectors.go` mixes unrelated domains (backup, profile, system metrics, urbit projection) into one large orchestration file.
evidence: `goseg/broadcast/collectors/collectors.go` is 971 lines and imports many unrelated domains (`backupsvc`, `click`, `network`, `startram`, `system`) in one module (lines 6-14).
The same file handles local backup filesystem scanning (lines 276-300), profile/startram parsing (lines 836-886), and hardware/drive state shaping (lines 890-953).

System-specific concerns are already substantial in dedicated modules (`goseg/system/disk.go`, `goseg/system/system.go`), but collector keeps re-aggregating those details, increasing cross-module coupling.
findings.suggestion: Split collector composition into domain-focused collectors (urbit, backup, profile, system) with a thin top-level assembler; keep filesystem and hardware-specific transformations in system/backup packages.

findings.summary: Transition routing uses repetitive hand-written mappings where data-driven registration would reduce duplication.
evidence: `goseg/rectify/rectify.go` repeats near-identical router entries for startram transitions (lines 324-348), each constructing the same `startramTransitionCommand` shape.

`goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go` contains many thin toggle wrappers that only forward transition IDs (lines 32-58, 102-104), plus near-parallel domain-step builders for urbit/minio alias updates (lines 106-165).
findings.suggestion: Introduce declarative transition registration tables (transition ID -> handler factory/step builder) and generate simple wrappers from shared descriptors to reduce parallel code paths.
